sentences.text: Yes.
Well, we decided we'd go to the United States.
So when the US embassy told us they'd grant us the visas, they said, how are you going to get there?
So we said, how we're going to get there?
We'll just have to get on a boat, and we'll cross the Atlantic and go to New York.
As I said, in those days, people didn't fly the way they do nowadays.
So they said, you can't do that.
In order to get to the Atlantic Ocean, you have to cross the North Sea, and the Germans have control of the North Sea.
You know, so we said, well, then what do we do?
They told us that we would have to go the other way.

We would have to go from Stockholm to Moscow, from Moscow to Vladivostok on the Trans-Siberian railroad, and then we'd have to cross to Japan and cross to Hawaii and into San Francisco and then to New York.
They said you have to have a sponsor.
Well, I don't think there'd be any problem with that because I had a cousin who was a rabbi in New York.
I knew if we got in touch with him there would be no problem.
And they said, you know what this is going to cost you?
No.
We don't know.
They said this is going to cost you a lot of money.
So we went to the Norwegian government.
And we told them this is what we want to do, but it looks a little impossible.
So they said-- asked, well, why don't you want to stay in Sweden?
So we told them.
We're just scared.
We don't know what is going to happen.
We would like to go to the United States.
They said, well, if you want to go, we will help you.
So I got in touch with my cousin in New York, and he agreed to give us all the papers that we needed.
And then Norwegian-- we didn't know how to go about that tremendous journey, so the Norwegian government made all the arrangements for us.
We would have to-- those days the DC-3 was the largest plane, so they made all the arrangements for us to travel right through to New York.
So we had our tickets and everything arranged, and we had our guarantee.
It took several weeks to get all these things arranged, you can imagine.
You know, when you lived in Europe in those days, America was New York.
You're going to New York.
You didn't go to America.
You went to New York, the Jewish people anyway, so there wasn't any thought that we weren't going to go all the way through.
Well, we had to leave Stockholm in the middle of winter to fly to Moscow, and we were at the airport-- it was a terribly tearful farewell for his mother because she knew she'd never see us again.
She was going to be left with her daughter in Stockholm.
We knew it would be the last time, so it was a terrible, terrible goodbye.
Went to the airport and they told us the plane could not take off because there were terrible storms in the Balkans, and the plane had to land to get fuel.
Because of the storms they would not be able to land.
So they didn't have enough fuel to fly directly to Moscow, so we would have to wait till the next day.
So we had to go back to my mother-in-law's again, and again leave the next day and the same crying, tearful farewell.
Plane wasn't going to leave that day either.
We had to go back for the third time.
And I remember my sister-in-law calling out when we came to the door, Mother, [NON-ENGLISH].
That means, they're coming again.
I can remember her standing there and calling, Mother, they're coming again.
Well, the third day when we got to the airport, they said, well, we're not going to take off today either.
There's no-- can't possibly land and get fuel.
So we immediately called the Swedish embassy then, told them-- the Norwegian government, the people who had helped us make all these arrangements.
And we told them what had happened and we're not going to be able to go.
Well, they said, you have to go because all the arrangements that you've made for you to go to New York, if you don't get to Moscow in time you're going to miss all the connections.
You have to go.
Well, we said, they won't let the planes-- they said, we're going to find out what's going on.
They made arrangements for the plane to take less people and more fuel and not land to get fuel, and that's what happened.
We came to Moscow.
We were supposed to be in Moscow for three days, but there was no time.
They made arrangements for an [INAUDIBLE] guy to meet us and we had to fill out some papers, some kind of formalities.
He took us someplace to get these all done.
We just got to the train, and they held up the train for two hours.
They said it had never happened before that they'd held the train up to waiting for two hours.
Now, let me tell you something.
You may think that we were very important people.
We were not.
We were just very ordinary people.
And the Norwegian government did this for us because they knew we wanted to get out, and they made all these arrangements.
So we got on the train in Moscow and we traveled across Moscow for seven days.
We were in third-class compartments, so other people in the compartments-- I had a-- oh yes, my baby was born in Stockholm, so I had a six-months-old baby with me.
And I fed him, I breastfed him.
And that's about all-- he was six months old, but that's about all the nourishment we had except that we-- when the train stopped at stations, we could always get a little food.
Like they had apples which I would-- I'd taken along a little scraper and scraped the apples and mashed some eggs.
And I had a little box like a little lunch box and I washed it in that.
And diapers, yes.
We had taken along-- friends in Stockholm had torn up all their sheets and when we told them what we planned to do, they said, well, how are you going to manage with diapers?
They tore up sheets and towels and things, and I had all these diapers which I could discard along the way because those days, you didn't have disposable diapers.
And so that's how we managed.
And we'd have seven days on the train and we got to Vladivostok.
In Vladivostok, there were many, many hundreds of refugees coming from all over Europe, and some of them didn't have any place to go from there.
They just got to Vladivostok and hoping they would get someplace.
We fortunately had all our reservations made.
So we stayed in Vladivostok a few days, and then we got on a Japanese ship to cross to Japan.

Now, the Norwegian government had told us that we would have to travel third class all the way because there was a limited amount of money here, but we would have to take first class on Vladivostok to Japan.
They said that's a terrible ship.
So we had first-class tickets.
I had a baby buggy with me which I carried with the baby to have it for the baby all the time.
And it was a ridiculous thing to take but you see things were different in those days.
It was a big buggy, a four-wheeled thing that didn't fold.
And I had this with me all along because I'd insisted how am I going to carry this baby around?
So that was with us.
And later on it got stolen in Los Angeles, but it's kind of incidental.
It's a rather strange thing to take a buggy all around the world and have it stolen in Los Angeles.
It was a big, white leatherette thing.
Well, we got on-- we were going to get on the boat in Vladivostok and, well, the boat was kind of docked just a little way away from the dock.
And to get onto it you had to go over a rope bridge with a couple-- we had three suitcases, mostly full of diapers because we didn't take any of our valuables with us.
That was all left in Stockholm.
Well, as long as I'm telling you, I'll tell you what happened to the valuables-- the silver mostly, but I'll go back and tell you.
We had to scrape up as much money as we could, possibly could.
We wanted to sell the silver, so we sold it to a man who said that he would keep this silver until one year after the war.
If he didn't hear from us within one year after the war, then it was his.
He could dispose of it.
But otherwise if we wanted to claim it, we should claim it within one year, and he would return it to us.
That was the arrangement, so, I mean, we hadn't taken any valuables with us.
OK, so we got on the boat over this rope bridge onto the boat, and these Japanese sailors were standing there.
Everybody downstair-- well, they didn't speak English.
In those days, the Japanese didn't speak English.
I mean, it was 50 years ago.
Everybody down, down, down, down.
So my husband, we have first-class tickets.
No, everybody down.
So we just-- you know, they were very rough, not polite.
Didn't say, please go downstairs.
Down, down, in Japanese.
OK, we came down there.
And what we saw was absolutely horrendous.
There were these Orthodox Jews with payots and the black clothing just jumped on us.
Food, food.
They had left your Poland and places where the Nazis had invaded.
They had been promised that they could get off the ship somewhere in the Pacific.
They would be taken in.
But when they got there on the boat, those islands had been taken over by the Vichy government.
They were French islands.
The Vichy government wouldn't take them in.
So these people were being shunted back and forth on this boat from Vladivostok to wherever the boat was going.
They were down there in this third-class place with-- and hardly being fed, and it was awful.
We couldn't do anything for them.
All we could do was try to share our food, which we didn't have.
We had to eat the food that they gave us.
But there were marked-off places about six feet by three feet for each passenger, and that's where we had supposed to be with a baby and the luggage and the buggy and-- they let us take the buggy.
Well, all we could do was speak a little Yiddish with them, and we couldn't speak-- they couldn't speak English.
We couldn't speak.
They just managed to escape.
We didn't know very much.
I mean, they were-- all they wanted was to exist.
I mean, everything was so horrifying that we didn't really have a chance.
They would stand there with their tefillin every morning and pray and pray to a merciful God, where is he?
OK, I hope God helped them eventually.
Well, we stayed-- they gave us three meals a day down there in the bottom of the boat there, just rice-- rice and some watery soup three meals a day.
So we just shared this with these people as much as we could.
Anyway, as soon as we got on the boat and we saw these terrible conditions, my husband went up there and asked to see the captain, because we had first-class tickets.
He asked to see the captain.
He explained to them there's a mistake.
We're not supposed to be down there.
We're supposed to have cabins.
So the captain-- so he says, I have a wife and baby down there.
So he said, well, bring your wife and baby up here.
So, of course, we thought they were going to give us the first-class cabin.
So my husband went down and collected our stuff and went up there, and the door to the captain's office was closed.
And there was a window so I held up the baby--
Oh.
I held up the baby, and the captain came to the door.
And he said, if you don't like it down there, you can jump in the ocean.
So there was nothing more to say obviously.
So we went down there and we stayed down there for-- the crossing took five days.
We stayed down there for three days, and then the soldiers-- the sailors came, and they said, all the first-class passengers can come up now.
So we collected all our stuff, and we went up there.
And they gave us a cabin, which we shared with two strangers, but that was OK.
Well, wasn't so OK but, I mean, you had to make it OK.
What could you do?
And then we were looking forward, maybe getting a meal in the dining room after all being half-starved down there.
So we sat down and they served us this rice, rice dish.
Was a little better than down below but maybe because it had some bits of dried fish in it, which we didn't get down below.
But it was terribly salty, terribly highly spiced.
So we asked the waiter there-- you know, they didn't give me any water.
We asked the waiter for a bottle of soda pop.
So right away, you know, open hand.
Here, give us some money.
So we gave him $2.
He brings us soda pop.
We had to pay him for the soda pop.
I think it was about $6.
It was a few dollars.
I forget exactly now.
He gave us the soda pop, and then he wanted a tip besides.
You know, this soda pop ended up by costing us $9.
I mean, this is 50 years ago.
That was a hell of a lot of money.
Shouldn't say that would.
But anyway it was a lot of money in those days.
And he poured this soda pop out in a glass right before me, and then I lifted it up to my lips.
He pulled it away.
He didn't let me drink it.
Just took it away.
Just took it away.
So what are you going to do?
Nothing.
Anyway, at the end of the fifth day we came to Japan.
We came to Japan and let's see.
Trying to think--
No, we had-- along the journey we had met a Swedish man, Mr.
Lindstrom.
Mr.
Lindstrom had come-- he was Swedish-born.
He had emigrated to America many years previously, and at a terribly unfortunate time he had decided to go back to Sweden to visit.
He didn't know that there was going to be all this trouble with Norway and Scandinavia being invaded.
And they were invaded while he was in Sweden.
He had to go-- he wanted to go back home to America.
He was an elderly man.
He was in his 70s.
And so he was traveling the same way as we were, and we met him.
But he was very scared.
He was alone, and he was scared.
So he kind of tagged onto us to be friendly with us and help him.
And so he had given us all his passports and his money and everything.
He didn't want to carry it because we had all our valuables tied to our bodies, so he gave them to us for safe keeping.
So we were with Mr.
Lindstrom when we came to Japan-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I'm trying to get this all straightened out.
Oh, no.
So we got on the-- we got on the train into Tokyo, that's it.
We got on the train, and we went into Tokyo.
And they made us put the buggy in the cargo compartment.
And when we got to Tokyo my husband had to go and retrieve the buggy, so we were-- Mr.
Lindstrom and the baby and myself, we were standing on the platform, and it was terribly, terribly windy.
So my husband said, you go into the waiting room, and I'll go get the buggy.
But we had the luggage standing there, and my husband said to Mr.
Lindstrom, you stay here with the luggage and don't move.
Don't go anywhere.
He said, you wait here until I come back.
And then my husband came into the waiting room.
He said, well, where's Mr.
Lindstrom?
He said, isn't Mr.
Lindstrom here?
I said, no, you left him on the platform.
Well, he said, he isn't there.
There's no luggage, and there's no Mr.
Lindstrom.
So we were running around the Tokyo airport there-- train station, I mean.
Tokyo train station looking for Mr.
Lindstrom, and he was gone.
In those days, the Japanese didn't speak English.
They finally found someone who said, yes, he'd seen a white man getting on another train.
Where's that train going?
It was going up to Yokohama.
So they called Yokohama, and Mr.
Lindstrom was at Yokohama.
My husband spoke to him on the phone.
He said, what happened to you?
I told you to stand on the platform.
He said, well, you told me not to leave the luggage, and they took the luggage and put it on the train.
So he said, I went off with the luggage, and the luggage went.
And we all went to Yokohama.
Oh my God.
We've got all his passports and money and everything.
He had our diapers.
And we said, you stay right there, my husband said.
Stay right where you are.
We're coming to Yokohama.
So we took the train, and we went to Yokohama.
Well, our boat to the United States had to leave from Yoko-- or to Hawaii.
Had to leave from Yokohama, so we decided we'd had enough now.
So we stayed in Yokohama.
We took him to a very nice hotel because after all we'd been through we thought we should get into a nice hotel.
We stayed at the hotel then.
After that we were through with Mr.
Lindstrom, I tell you.
So we stayed at the hotel in Yokohama, and my baby started to cry and got sick.
We called for a doctor and an English-speaking doctor, happened to be American.
It was amazing.
He came, and he examined the baby.
And he said, he just cut his first tooth, and everything's going to all right.
So he cut his fist tooth in Yokohama.
We stayed there about three days.
And then we got another Japanese ship, which was a great deal better than the last one.
It was not a cruise ship by any means-- I don't know if they had cruise ships in those days.
But we were third-class, and I would walk on the deck with the baby.
And one day a lady came over to me.
And she said that she was in first class, and she said, I've seen you walking on the deck with your baby.
And she said, would you like to come to my cabin and take a bath?
I went, oh my goodness, how must I look?
Because she said, I understand you don't have private facilities here down in third class.
Oh, I said, thanks.
I said, you must be an angel sent from heaven.
And I went to her cabin, bathed the baby and bathed myself.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] And third class we just-- it was fairly primitive.
We ate at long tables.
Well, they fed us OK.
So, I mean, it was nothing really to object to.
There were a lot of people on that ship who didn't have visas to the United States.
They were going to South America, and we were envied.
I tell you, we got to the point where we didn't dare tell them that we were going to the United States because they were jealous.
That's where everybody wanted to go.
So we landed at San Francisco.
Now, we had tickets to go to New York.
We hadn't thought of going any place but New York, but there was a letter waiting for us.
Some mail was delivered onto the ship, letter for us from my cousin, the rabbi in New York.
And he, in this letter, he advised us not to come to New York.
He said everybody wants to go to California.
He said, you're there in California.
Why don't you stay there?
He said, maybe you'll have good opportunities there.
Now, he said, you have a cousin in Los Angeles.
Now, I'd heard of this cousin.
I knew I had a cousin but, you know, just vaguely.
I knew that I had a cousin, but I couldn't have told you his name.
And I couldn't have told you his address and anything about him.
I just vaguely knew of him.
He wrote this-- my cousin Joshua wrote me his name and address, and he said, why don't you get in touch with him because he's in the same business as your husband.
Maybe he can give you some advice.
Well, we took this as a sign that maybe they didn't want their poor refugees coming to New York.
I mean, three people coming to New York without money, without anything.
Maybe they didn't want us.
So we said, what the heck?
We've come this far on our own.
We'll try California.
So I immediately wrote a letter to my cousin in Los Angeles and told him the situation.
And he immediately got in touch with us and said, yes.
He said, I've heard of you, he said, vaguely, the same as you've heard me vaguely.
He said, but come to Los Angeles.
He said, if I can do anything to help you I'll be glad to do this.
So we got on the train-- by this time I think we had one suitcase.
We disposed of our diapers all along the way and the baby clothes, and that's about all we had.
I think I was wearing the same dress-- I remember my dress had a hole in the sleeve.
I came to Los Angeles with an old, dirty dress and a hole in the sleeve.
And they came to meet us at the train station.
Now, they had tried to figure out how old I could be.
They knew approximately how old I could be and they figured, well, if she has a son.
He could be anywhere from one year to 10 years.
They didn't know-- I hadn't written the age of my son.
He was only six months.
So they didn't know what to expect, and here I come with a little baby in my arms and a baby buggy.
And they were absolutely astounded when they saw this baby.
You mean you traveled all this way and everything with that little baby?
Yes.
Well, they were wonderful to us-- still are.
And they took us to their home that night, and we stayed at their home.
And the next day Michael went out and found an apartment, and the next day he went out and found a job for $17 a week.
But let me tell you, it was never our intention to stay in the United States because we just come here as a refuge.
We always thought that after the war, we always thought we'll go home.
But my husband was-- we stayed here, he progressed to a job for $25 a week.
And then he got a letter from Uncle Sam saying that-- well, this was six months before Pearl Harbor, so 1940-- you know, United States was at war.

And he got a letter from the United States saying that he was drafted, but because he was not a United States citizen, he would not have to serve in the United-- he could refuse to serve in the United States Army.
Well, we had never intended to stay in the United States, so here was a question.
What should we do?
Just treat this country as a haven and then go back to Norway or--
He said, no.
My husband said, no way.
He said, this country took me in, and they're going to fight the Germans.
And I'm going too.
So he joined the United States Army, was sent to the Philippines.
But he was never in active duty.
He was sent to the Philippines, and I was-- lived then-- then in 1942, I had my second son.
So I was left here with two small children.
Well, I had his-- what did he get?
I think the United States Army gave me $125 a month.
I remember the Red Cross came to visit me and asked me-- told me that if I needed anything, I said I'd try and manage on $125 a month.
I took in a little work.
Remember there were shells, there was a firm I saw advertised, a firm had these shells that they would look for homeworkers to string them as necklaces.
And I used to do that until the war in the Pacific.
Then they couldn't import the shells anymore.
But I got along.
I had a pension of $125-- not pension.
You know, the soldier's pay.
And then he used to send me some of his pay that he got.
You know, he got a pay, and he said he never spent it.
He just sent it to me.
And I got along.
And 1945-- and in 1945 he came back.
He survived the war, thank goodness.
And then he-- after the war years, things were good in the United States.
They were pretty good.
And he was a hard worker, diligent worker, and we got along fine.
And then the letters-- then when Norway started-- and we were always thinking, should we go back or should we stay?
And then Norway started to settle down, and things started to be prosperous in Norway too.
And then we got letters from Norway saying, you have to come home.
This is where you belong, and you have to come home.
You can't stay.
What's left of this family has got to stay together.
But by this time we'd established ourselves in the United States.
We were all-- my husband become an American citizen.
I became an American citizen-- because he became an American citizen automatically after three months in the United States Army.
I had to go to school and study and became an American citizen.
I had a son who became an American citizen.
My other son was born here.
Here we were, all American citizens.
Now we're going to go back to Norway and start all over?
We knew we'd have to go and start all over again.
We'd come back to Norway with nothing, and then what?
And we liked California.
We thought to go back in the cold, you know?
So we decided we would stay.
By this time had you already found out what happened to the family members on your husband's side of the family that were deported or killed?
Yeah, soon after the end of the war.
Yeah, they started to investigate and they were-- you know, the Germans kept very meticulous records.
They could tell you what-- the fate of every single person.
And they were all sent to Auschwitz-- three small children, four small children.
The loss of the children was a-- well, of course, the whole thing was a heartache because I was very closely attached to this family.
The loss of the children was a terribly hard heartache for me because while we were living in Norway, the parents of three of the children had said that they wanted to go on a vacation.
They wanted to.
They were going to take a boat up to the north part of Norway and they could leave the children with a maid or somebody.
But they made a suggestion that they would send the children up to a little farm, a little resort farm that was not too far outside of [INAUDIBLE].
And they said would you like to go with the children and stay with them?
They said, we can't send them alone.
Would you like to go and stay with the children there for a couple of weeks?
And you'll have a vacation.
We'll pay for everything.
And it's close enough that Michael could come over the weekend and stay with us.
And oh, gee, that would be-- why not?
Why shouldn't I do that?
So--
